finding: Judgments that an agent brought about a negative side effect intentionally depend on moral valence, not just causal knowledge; agents receive higher intentionality ratings for harmful outcomes they foresaw but did not pursue than for helpful outcomes, independent of pragmatic factors.

Bureaucratic responsiveness to constituent requests does not vary by race (no discrimination against blacks), but response tone (named greeting) shows discrimination against Hispanic names; effect is attenuated in high-minority communities but persists even when responding official is Hispanic.

Visual judgment of body weight exhibits contraction bias: heavier bodies are underestimated and lighter bodies overestimated relative to population mean; discrimination thresholds increase with stimulus magnitude (Weber's law), making weight gain harder to detect in obese individuals.

Students believing intelligence is malleable show steeper upward grade trajectories over junior high transition than those believing intelligence is fixed; effect is mediated by learning goals, positive effort beliefs, low helpless attributions, and positive coping strategies.

Brief intervention teaching malleable intelligence reverses declining grade trajectories in low-achieving students; effect is strongest for students with initially fixed mindset and operates through increased classroom motivation and effort, measurable by teacher observation within one semester.

Three-dimensional scene-based orientation permits preattentive (parallel, rapid) visual search comparable to image-based orientation, suggesting early visual system extracts volumetric properties; effect depends on complete system of spatial relations (line junctions), not local features.
